item.title: How I use Highland Fling
item.description:
Fling is a brand new feature for Highland Pro that addresses a long-simmering frustration: getting feedback on what you’ve written. Whenever I finish a draft, I want to give it to some trusted people to read — both for comments and general proofreading. In the old days, that generally meant printing and handing it to […]

The post How I use Highland Fling first appeared on John August.
item.source:markdown:
Fling is a brand new feature for Highland Pro that addresses a long-simmering frustration: getting feedback on what you’ve written. Whenever I finish a draft, I want to give it to some trusted people to read — both for comments and general proofreading. In the old days, that generally meant printing and handing it to \[…\]
The post [How I use Highland Fling](https://johnaugust.com/2026/how-i-use-highland-fling) first appeared on [John August](https://johnaugust.com).

item.title: The World’s Most Prolific Public Domain Publisher (Sec. 105)
item.description: If the work of the state is owned by the people as work made for hire, then all of the work of the state is owned by the people—not just the laws, but reports, literary works, sound recordings, photographs, and even possibly academic research. The court understandably would have been reluctant to hand down an opinion that was so shattering to expectations. But it also would have been the best decision. Taxpayers, after all, foot the entire bill for their government.
item.source:markdown: If the work of the state is owned by the people as work made for hire, then all of the work of the state is owned by the people—not just the laws, but reports, literary works, sound recordings, photographs, and even possibly academic research. The court understandably would have been reluctant to hand down an opinion that was so shattering to expectations. But it also would have been the best decision. Taxpayers, after all, foot the entire bill for their government.

item.title: Beats to Scenes with Drew Goddard
item.description:
John welcomes back Drew Goddard (Project Hail Mary, High Potential) to ask, how do you turn the beats of your story into full scenes? Using Drew’s script for The Martian, we look at how he translates moments in a book into scenes in a movie, the freedom Drew finds in a beat sheet, how beats […]

The post Beats to Scenes with Drew Goddard first appeared on John August.
item.source:markdown:
John welcomes back Drew Goddard (Project Hail Mary, High Potential) to ask, how do you turn the beats of your story into full scenes? Using Drew’s script for The Martian, we look at how he translates moments in a book into scenes in a movie, the freedom Drew finds in a beat sheet, how beats \[…\]
The post [Beats to Scenes with Drew Goddard](https://johnaugust.com/2026/beats-to-scenes-with-drew-goddard) first appeared on [John August](https://johnaugust.com).

item.title: Library and Archives 101: AI and the False Promise of Control
item.description: This is the first in a series of essays about the choices that knowledge institutions face as AI reshapes how their collections and resources are used. The people on various sides of these debates are, for the most part, deeply committed to access to knowledge — the disagreement is about means. Some believe that the best way to protect access to knowledge is to impose new conditions on how AI can use it and for what purposes. I think that approach is more likely to undermine access than to save it. This post in particular is about the risk of libraries and archives reimagining themselves as gatekeepers of AI-era access. To help contain these essays, we will be posting them as a series titled “AI & Access to Knowledge.”
item.source:markdown: This is the first in a series of essays about the choices that knowledge institutions face as AI reshapes how their collections and resources are used. The people on various sides of these debates are, for the most part, deeply committed to access to knowledge — the disagreement is about means. Some believe that the best way to protect access to knowledge is to impose new conditions on how AI can use it and for what purposes. I think that approach is more likely to undermine access than to save it. This post in particular is about the risk of libraries and archives reimagining themselves as gatekeepers of AI-era access. To help contain these essays, we will be posting them as a series titled “AI & Access to Knowledge.”

item.title: How do bad movies get made? (Encore)
item.description:
John and Craig revisit a favorite episode focused on a single topic: bad movies and how they happen. Using first-hand experience, they look at how bad ideas make it to the screen, how good ideas go wrong, and the range of patterns that end in terrible movies. We also make a list of our then-dream […]

The post How do bad movies get made? (Encore) first appeared on John August.
item.source:markdown:

item.title: Free Work
item.description:
John and Craig tackle the scourge of free work and offer strategies to avoid it. They look at established norms, potential remedies, the things that should work but often don’t, and how writers should think about the time and effort they’re putting in before getting paid. We also follow up on useful comps, AI coverage, […]

The post Free Work first appeared on John August.
item.source:markdown:
John and Craig tackle the scourge of free work and offer strategies to avoid it. They look at established norms, potential remedies, the things that should work but often don’t, and how writers should think about the time and effort they’re putting in before getting paid. We also follow up on useful comps, AI coverage, \[…\]
The post [Free Work](https://johnaugust.com/2026/free-work) first appeared on [John August](https://johnaugust.com).
